summary: The Chinese government has added the Canadian semiconductor consultancy TechInsights to its 'unreliable entity list,' banning it from doing business in China. This action was triggered by TechInsights' reverse-engineering reports claiming that Huawei's AI processors (Ascend) and other chips (Kirin) were using components from sanctioned firms like TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix, or were produced by SMIC at 7nm, despite US sanctions.
main_claims: China's Ministry of Commerce has blacklisted TechInsights to safeguard national sovereignty and security.
TechInsights' reports claim Huawei used advanced components from TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix in its Ascend AI processors.
TechInsights' reports claim SMIC produced 7nm chips for Huawei's Mate 60 Pro and Kirin X90, but faced challenges advancing to 5nm.
The blacklisting of a Western consultancy is an escalation in the US-China tech war.
evidence_or_facts: TechInsights was added to the 'unreliable entity list' by China's Ministry of Commerce.
Chinese individuals and organizations are told not to transmit data or sensitive information to the firm.
TechInsights published reports on the Ascend 910B and Kirin 9020 chipset.
SMIC was identified as the foundry for the 7nm Kirin processor in the Mate 60 Pro.
TechInsights is a Canadian-based consultancy with subsidiaries in the US, Europe, and South Korea.
The US Department of Commerce added 12 Chinese companies to its Entity List regarding weaponized drones.
rhetorical_frames: jarak between US sanctions and actual chip procurement procurement
Reverse-engineering as a 'risk' to national security
state_and_policy_content: Use of the 'unreliable entity list' by China's Ministry of Commerce as a trade blacklist.
Directives to Chinese entities to not share data with TechInsights.
US Department of Commerce Entity List used to target companies facilitating the purchase of US components for drones.
capital_accumulation_content: Huawei's attempts to maintain production of advanced AI processors and Ascend chips despite US sanctions.
Huawei's use of (allegedly) smuggled or third-party sourced advanced components from TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix.
infrastructure_and_supply_chain_content: The role of semiconductor foundries (TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix, SMIC) in the global chip supply chain.
The role of 'teardown' reports in tracking the source of components in the same supply chain.
Challenges in advancing from 7nm to 5nm manufacturing processes.
geopolitical_competition_content: Citing of 'military and technological cooperation with Taiwan' as a reason for blacklisting.
The use of trade blacklists as tools of geopolitical competition.
possible_theory_hooks: The role of third-party intelligence/consultancies in monitoring sanctions compliance
Information asymmetry in global supply chains
what_is_missing_for_theory_evaluation: Confirmation of whether Huawei actually used TSMC/Samsung/SK Hynix chips (denials exist).
Detailed financial data on the costs of these procurement strategies.
Specific legal mechanisms used by China to actually enforce the 'unreliable entity list' against a foreign consultancy.
